sessions.title: Contributing to Production
sessions.subtitle: Collaborating with developers & contributing to production

slides.title: Contributing to Production
slides.subtitle: Collaborating with developers & contributing to production

slides.title: Framing
slides.subtitle: Contributing to production at AI speed — without breaking the team's trust
points: AI removes the barrier to writing code — but production systems have standards, conventions, and history that AI doesn't automatically know
The new constraint isn't coding ability; it's understanding how to collaborate effectively with development teams and existing codebases
This session covers the workflow, discipline, and context-setting that separates useful AI contributions from noise
What to know → How to work with developers → How to ship with confidence

slides.title: Tinytoolstack Demo
slides.subtitle: The example project we'll use throughout this session
points: A real production codebase
Create a new application live
Design Token Brief

slides.title: Rules for Production Projects
slides.subtitle: The discipline that separates a contribution from a liability
points: Prompt-requests: treat each AI-assisted task as a unit of work with a clear scope — open-ended requests create open-ended (unpredictable) output
Small changes: make one change at a time, ask the agent to commit after each logical unit — small diffs are reviewable, large diffs are accepted on trust
Traceability: always link your work to a ticket — 'why does this exist?' should always have an answer, and 'Claude made it' is not an answer
MCP with Jira/Linear: let the agent pull context from your project management system — no manual copy-paste of requirements into every prompt
slides.tip: A change without a ticket is untraceable. A commit message without context is a question that will be asked in a post-mortem.

slides.title: Managing Context
slides.subtitle: Context is the product. Manage it like one.
points: Context rot: every token you add to context has a cost — provide only what's strictly relevant, or the agent's performance degrades mid-task
Review changes: don't accept AI output uncritically — use a second agent with a clean, explicit context to review what the first one produced
Compact aggressively: start a new session for a new task rather than carrying the full history of a previous one into it
slides.tip: You don't write better prompts. You build better context.

slides.title: Writing PRDs with an Agent
slides.subtitle: The spec the agent implements against — written with the agent, not handed to it
points: Use the agent as a thinking partner, not a document generator — start with your rough idea, then ask it to surface missing requirements, edge cases, and conflicting assumptions before a line of code is written
Structure that works: problem statement, target user and their context, success criteria (how will you know it worked?), explicit scope and non-scope, open questions
Challenge your own PRD: 'What's the weakest assumption here?', 'What would a user try that we haven't handled?', 'What does this spec leave ambiguous for an engineer reading it?'
The PRD becomes the session context — paste or link it at the start of every implementation session so the agent knows the goal, not just the task
slides.tip: A PRD written with an agent surfaces questions you forgot to ask — the agent has no familiarity with 'how we usually do it', so it asks what a first-time reader would.

slides.title: Plan First, Execute Second
slides.subtitle: Expensive judgment, cheap execution
points: Use a more capable model (Opus 4.7) to design the approach, then a faster model (Sonnet 4.6) to execute — don't pay for judgment you don't need on every line
Behaviour-Driven Development (BDD): write scenarios in plain language first — agent implements to a precise, human-readable spec
Specification-Driven Development (SDD): define the interface and expected behaviour before the implementation — reduces ambiguity, improves output reliability

slides.title: The Development Harness
slides.subtitle: The infrastructure that makes AI contributions reliable
points: Hooks: automated actions that run before and after agent operations — linting, formatting, and security checks run without you asking
Permissions: least-privilege access scoped per task — an agent working on UI shouldn't be able to touch database migrations
Skills: reusable slash commands that encode your team's best workflows — /tidy, /review, /commit — so the agent behaves consistently on every session
CI/CD pipeline as the final gate: linter, tests, and E2E checks catch what agent review misses — quality gates that don't rely on human diligence
slides.tip: The harness is what makes AI contributions production-safe. Without it, you're trusting the agent. With it, you're trusting the system.

slides.title: Preparation & Onboarding
slides.subtitle: The context that runs before you say a word
points: Alignment is provided by the development team: tech stack, conventions, libraries, and design patterns — the CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md file is the entry point the agent reads at session start
Vision: what the project is trying to achieve and for whom — an agent that understands the goal makes better tradeoff decisions without being told
Once alignment is defined, the prompt isn't that important — you don't explain every task from scratch to your best employee; they're context-aware and need one sentence, not a manual
slides.tip: A well-maintained CLAUDE.md is the highest-leverage investment in AI collaboration. It compounds on every future session.

slides.title: Specialised Agents & Feedback Loops
slides.subtitle: Scope the agent to the task, close the loop automatically
points: Skills and sub-agents: specialised agents for specific roles (e.g., a React specialist with only frontend context) — scope the agent to the task, not the whole codebase
Computer-use and Playwright MCP: let the agent interact with the browser and get automated feedback from the running app — close the feedback loop without manual testing
Sub-agent boundaries also enforce least-privilege: a frontend agent with no access to database migrations can't accidentally touch them

slides.title: Debugging with AI
slides.subtitle: Using agents when things break — without losing hours to trial and error
points: Feed the full error trace, not a summary — agents diagnose from exact stack traces, file paths, and line numbers; paraphrased errors lose the signal that matters

Rubber duck prompting: explain the problem to the agent as if it knows nothing — the act of articulating what should be happening versus what is happening often surfaces the bug before the agent even replies
Bisect strategy: ask the agent 'what changed between the last working state and now?' — narrow the search space before asking for a fix, or you'll get a fix for the wrong problem
Isolate before you fix: paste the minimal failing snippet, not the entire file — agents produce better diagnoses on a 20-line reproduction than on a 500-line context dump
slides.tip: If the agent's first fix doesn't work, don't just retry — ask it to explain its reasoning first. Wrong fixes usually come from wrong assumptions about the root cause, not from bad code.

slides.title: Getting Your Dev Team on Board
slides.subtitle: The resistance is real — and usually justified
points: Non-determinism is a fair concern — but developers are also not deterministic, and the same argument for consistent, reviewable output applies to human work too
The real problem is usually alignment investment: if CLAUDE.md, conventions, and quality gates aren't set up, AI output can't meet the bar — that's a setup problem, not an AI problem
Since Sonnet 4.6 and Codex 5.2, there has been a step change in output quality and capability — resistance based on experiences with earlier models may be outdated

The best alignment is written by the people who own the domain: a test engineer encodes what good tests look like into a testing skill or sub-agent; a backend developer defines API conventions; a security engineer writes the security review hook — each role makes the AI permanently smarter in their area
slides.tip: Example: the test engineer doesn't just describe good tests in a doc — they write a /create-tests skill that embeds coverage rules, naming conventions, and edge-case patterns, so every agent session gets their expertise automatically.

slides.title: The Process That Earns Trust
slides.subtitle: Show the gates, not just the speed
points: Existing code quality matters: agents replicate the patterns they see — a codebase with consistent, clean conventions produces better AI output than a tangled one
CI/CD with quality gates (linter, tests, E2E) is the handshake: developers approve the process, not individual AI lines — they trust the gate, not the generator
The strongest argument for AI adoption isn't speed — it's that good AI setup forces the team to document what 'good' looks like, and that benefits everyone
slides.tip: Don't demo the output. Demo the process — the gates, the review step, the traceability. That's what a developer can trust.

slides.title: Dev Handoff
slides.subtitle: Close out work the way a good engineer would
points: Let the agent generate lightweight documentation — arc42 sections, README updates, architecture decision records — as part of the task, not as an afterthought
The agent should generate a PR description that explains what changed and why — reviewers shouldn't have to reconstruct intent from a diff
A well-written PR is a forcing function: if the agent can't explain what it did and why, the scope was probably too broad
Traceability closes the loop: ticket → PR → commit → documentation — any link missing is a liability in production

slides.title: The Future Team
slides.subtitle: Smaller teams, higher leverage, faster compounding
points: The teams that invest in alignment — vision, context, skills, and infrastructure — compound their advantage on every subsequent task
Smaller 3-4 person teams with strong AI collaboration outperform traditional larger teams on well-defined problem spaces
The focus shifts to building and deeply understanding the domain — AI handles mechanical execution, humans handle judgment and discovery
The bottleneck is no longer headcount; it's the quality of the context your team has built and maintains

slides.title: Key Takeaways
slides.subtitle: What to carry out of this session
points: Production contributions require production discipline: small changes, linked tickets, committing after each logical unit
Context quality is the product: provide only what's relevant, review AI output, plan with the best model and execute with the fastest
The harness — hooks, permissions, skills, and CI/CD gates — is what makes AI contributions safe without constant human vigilance
Alignment is built once and compounds on every session: CLAUDE.md, vision, skills, sub-agents — invest here first
PRDs with an agent: draft the spec collaboratively before any code — use the agent to challenge assumptions, surface edge cases, and structure success criteria
Debugging with AI: feed full error traces, use rubber duck prompting to surface assumptions, bisect to narrow scope before asking for a fix
Getting your dev team on board means demonstrating the process, not just the output — show the gates, not just the speed
The future advantage is organisational: teams that invest in shared context and alignment will outperform those running individual AI in isolation

slides.title: Q & A
slides.subtitle: Thank you for joining

sessions.title: Validate Like a PM, Ship Like an Engineer
sessions.subtitle: Analytics, behaviour tracking, validation, and AI-assisted product decisions

slides.title: The Throughline
slides.subtitle: Building is now cheap. Validation is the bottleneck.
points: AI makes execution faster and cheaper — which means the quality of your decisions matters more, not less
Bad decisions just ship faster. This session is about raising decision quality while moving at AI speed.
What to measure → How to measure it → How fast to move
What questions to ask → What traps to avoid → How to find demand you can't see yet

slides.title: Outcome vs. Behaviour Metrics
slides.subtitle: The insight is in the behaviour, not the outcome
points: Outcome metrics tell you what happened — conversion, retention, revenue
Behaviour metrics tell you why — the sequence of actions that led to the outcome
Analytics tells you what users did. It doesn't tell you what they wanted to do but couldn't.
The gap between 'what users do' and 'what users want' is where product decisions live
slides.tip: Instrument before you ship. The first week of data — often the most important — is gone if you add tracking retroactively.

slides.title: What to Instrument
slides.subtitle: Five categories that cover most product insight
points: Entry points — how did they arrive? (channel, referrer, campaign)
Activation events — the first action that correlates with long-term retention
Friction points — where do users stop, slow down, or retry?
Feature usage — which features are actually used, by whom, how often
Exit events — the last action before churning

slides.title: Behaviour Tracking in AI Products
slides.subtitle: Traditional analytics misses what matters most
points: Query tracking — what are users asking the agent? What categories emerge?
Rephrasing attempts — how many times does a user restate the same request? (= friction signal)
Fallback-to-manual — when does AI fail and the user does it themselves?
Abandonment after AI response — user got an answer and left; was it useful or useless?
slides.tip: These signals are invisible to traditional analytics but are the richest source of product insight in AI products.

slides.title: The Validation Ladder
slides.subtitle: Reducing uncertainty before committing resources
points: Belief → the assumption behind your current decision
Hypothesis → the testable version of that belief
Signal → the cheapest evidence that would update your belief
Conviction → enough evidence to commit resources
slides.tip: AI accelerates every step of this ladder — but it doesn't replace the thinking at each level.

slides.title: Using AI in Validation Workflows
slides.subtitle: Synthesis, hypothesis generation, and interview analysis at scale
points: Generating hypotheses: 'What are 5 assumptions we haven't validated? Which is most likely to be wrong?'
Synthesising qualitative data: upload interview transcripts → cluster themes, surface contradictions
Paste support tickets → identify top friction points and unexpected use cases
Interview analysis: feed transcripts to Claude — 'What did users say that surprised them? What workarounds are they using?'

slides.title: Speed vs. Rigor
slides.subtitle: A function of reversibility and stakes — not a values debate
points: Fast and wrong is expensive. Slow and right is also expensive.
Ask two questions: How reversible is this decision? What breaks if you're wrong?
Reversible + low stakes → ship fast and learn. Irreversible + high stakes → high rigor, no shortcuts.
Shared language with engineering: 'This is a Category 1 — ship dirty and measure. That one is Category 3 — slow down.'
slides.tip: 'If I'm wrong about this, how long does it take to undo it and what does it cost?' — If the answer is 'a day and nothing', ship.

slides.title: Three Decision Categories
slides.subtitle: Calibrate rigor to the type of decision
points: Category 1 — Latency-sensitive: landing page copy, onboarding flow, UI variations. Ship fast, learn fast.
Category 2 — Trust-sensitive: agents acting on behalf of users, health/legal advice, personal data. Validate before shipping.
Category 3 — Infrastructure: data model, auth architecture, agent orchestration, API contracts. High rigor, slow down.
Category 3 decisions are irreversible in practice — 'quick and dirty' here creates years of debt

slides.title: The PM Superpower
slides.subtitle: AI handles the how. You own the what.
points: AI can synthesise interviews, analyse data, generate hypotheses, A/B test copy, and prototype in hours
What AI cannot do: decide which beliefs are worth testing and in what order
That judgment — what to validate next — is the highest-leverage PM skill in the agent era
Level 1: What do we believe? Level 2: What would disprove it? Level 3: What's the cheapest test?

slides.title: The Uncomfortable Question Protocol
slides.subtitle: The best validation question is the one you don't want to ask
points: 'Do you actually need this feature, or would you just use it if it existed?'
'If this product went away tomorrow, what would you do instead?'
'Would you pay for this if it wasn't free?'
'Have you ever actually used the feature we're building around?'
slides.tip: For every major product decision: state the belief explicitly, then ask the question you least want users to answer honestly — and ask that question.

slides.title: Anti-patterns: Using AI to Confirm What You Already Believe
slides.subtitle: AI is helpful and agreeable by default — if you prompt with your conclusion baked in, you'll get confirmation
points: The leading prompt: 'Our users really value speed — suggest ways to highlight it.' Fix: ask what users care about, then test the assumption.
Survivorship bias: AI pattern-matches to successful products, not your specific users and market
Validating the wrong assumption: users said they want it, but won't change their workflow to get it
Moving fast past uncomfortable signals: running a test and ignoring a slightly negative result
slides.tip: Define your decision rule before running the test. Write it down. Hold to it.

slides.title: Red-Teaming Your Product with AI
slides.subtitle: If an AI conversation isn't challenging your assumptions, something is wrong
points: Pre-mortem: 'Imagine it's 6 months from now. This feature failed. What happened?'
Failed user narrative: 'You are a user who tried this and stopped after 2 weeks. Walk me through what happened.'
Contrarian investor: 'You are an investor who passed on funding this. What was your reasoning?'
Explicit pushback: 'What's the strongest case against this?' or 'Argue against this feature decision.'

slides.title: Latent Demand in the Agent Era
slides.subtitle: Demand suppressed by friction, unfamiliarity, or lack of vocabulary
points: When agents drop friction 10x: tasks that were 'not worth it' become trivial, problems users learned to live with become solvable
Signal types: awkward asks, edge-case workarounds, unexpected usage patterns, 'Can it also...?' questions
The question you're not letting users ask is the question that will reveal latent demand
Anti-pattern: building only for expressed demand — nobody asked for the iPod; they wanted 1000 songs in their pocket

slides.title: AI-First Product Design Patterns
slides.subtitle: When execution is near-free, the product assumptions change
points: The 10x friction question: 'What would our users do with this if it was 10x faster and 10x cheaper?'
Designing for delegation, not just automation — user hands off judgment, not just a task
Confirmation steps: show the plan before execution, especially for irreversible actions
Interrupt points, trust calibration, explainability on demand: 'Why did you do that?' should always have an answer

slides.title: Why a Design System?
slides.subtitle: AI writes UI fast. Without a design system, inconsistency compounds just as fast.
points: Without a design system, AI-generated UI diverges with every new feature — different spacing, different patterns, different names for the same thing
With a design system, every component is predictable, composable, and correct by default
Design systems reduce the decision surface for AI agents — fewer wrong choices, fewer hallucinated patterns
A well-documented component library is prompt-ready: the agent knows exactly what exists and how to use it
slides.tip: A design system is now as much infrastructure for AI agents as it is for human developers.

slides.title: MUI or shadcn/ui?
slides.subtitle: Both are production-grade and agent-friendly — the choice is about philosophy
points: MUI (Material UI): comprehensive, opinionated, Google Material Design — extensive props, great TypeScript support, rapid development
shadcn/ui: copy-paste components built on Radix + Tailwind — fully customisable, you own the code, no version lock-in
MUI: faster to start, steeper to customise to a unique brand; shadcn: full control, slightly more initial setup
Both are actively maintained, accessible at their core, and familiar to AI agents trained on public documentation
slides.tip: Full brand control and ownership → shadcn/ui. Speed and coverage → MUI. Both compound well over time.

slides.title: Storybook: Your Living Documentation
slides.subtitle: Every component, isolated, tested, and described — for humans and for agents
points: Storybook renders components in isolation — outside your app, without side effects, in every possible state
Each story = one state of one component: default, loading, error, empty, disabled, with long text
Stories serve as both visual tests and specifications — if it's in Storybook, it's documented behaviour
Agents can use Storybook stories as context: the full prop surface and every state, without reading source code
Storybook a11y addon runs axe-core on every story automatically — accessibility regressions caught at the component level
slides.tip: A component without a Storybook story isn't a component — it's a guess. Agents will guess wrong.

slides.title: Accessibility for the EU
slides.subtitle: EAA 2025 mandates WCAG 2.1 AA compliance — legal requirement, not best practice
points: EU Accessibility Act (EAA) came into force June 2025 — most digital products serving EU users must comply
WCAG 2.1 AA: keyboard navigation, screen reader support, colour contrast ratios, focus management, no motion traps
Radix UI (under shadcn/ui) handles the hard parts: ARIA roles, focus traps, keyboard events, announcements — out of the box
MUI has deep a11y investment: every component ships with correct ARIA and keyboard behaviour by default
Your job: don't override the accessibility built in. Test with axe-core or the Storybook a11y addon on every component.
slides.tip: Build with Radix-based components and you start from AA compliant. Override Radix's defaults and you break compliance.

slides.title: Delivering an Agent-Ready Library
slides.subtitle: A design system an AI can use without guessing or hallucinating
points: Every component has a Storybook story covering every prop, every state, and every variant — no undocumented behaviour
Prop names are consistent and predictable across components — an agent that knows Button can infer IconButton
Component names map directly to design intent: Button not Btn, Heading not H1Wrapper, Avatar not UserCircle
Export a tokens file (colours, spacing, typography scales) — agents use tokens, not raw hex values or pixel counts
Write a design-system.md: the philosophy, naming conventions, what not to do, and how to request new components
slides.tip: The test: can an AI agent build a new page using only your components and documentation — without asking questions? If yes, the library is agent-ready.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
slides.subtitle: What to carry out of this session
points: Analytics tells you what happened. Behaviour tracking tells you why. AI telemetry tells you what users wanted but couldn't get.
Speed vs. rigor isn't a values debate — it's a function of reversibility and stakes
The PM superpower is knowing what to validate, not how. AI handles the how.
The most valuable validation questions are the ones you don't want to ask
AI-speed development makes inconsistency compound faster — a design system is infrastructure for agents, not just humans
Roadmap question: 'What would users do if this was free of friction, effort, and time?' That gap is your roadmap.

sessions.title: Aligning Humans and Agents Across an Organisation
sessions.subtitle: Making AI adoption compound at the team level, not just the individual level

slides.title: The Throughline
slides.subtitle: Individual AI adoption doesn't compound — productivity gains stay personal, not organisational
points: Most teams have individuals who are great with AI and organisations that aren't
The gap: when a person leaves or switches projects, their best workflows leave with them
This session is about making alignment distributable — shared context, shared skills, shared governance
The goal: one improvement benefits every agent session across the org, not just the person who made it

slides.title: Three Layers of Alignment Distribution
slides.subtitle: How shared AI context reaches every team member without a meeting
points: Plugin (org-wide): shared Claude configuration, custom skills, slash commands, curated permissions — the standard everyone starts from
Repo (project-wide): canonical CLAUDE.md templates, shared memory, project archetypes — context specific to each codebase
MCP (system-wide): structured access to internal systems — Notion, Jira, Slack, internal APIs — at call time, not baked into context
slides.tip: Each layer has a different owner and a different lifespan. Plugin changes are org-wide. Repo changes are per-project. MCP access changes are per-system.

slides.title: The Company Knowledge Base
slides.subtitle: Three things agents — and new hires — need to be effective from day one
points: How we work: norms, decision-making rituals, communication standards — the unwritten rules, written down
What we're building: glossary, domain context, architecture decisions — the shared vocabulary that prevents agents from hallucinating your domain
How we use agents: trusted workflows, known failure modes, what agents should never do autonomously
slides.tip: If a new hire would be confused by it, an agent will be too. The knowledge base is the onboarding doc that both can read.

slides.title: Onboarding With Agents
slides.subtitle: A new hire should be productive with an agent in week one
points: The bottleneck for new hires and new agents is identical: lack of shared context about how the organisation works
The knowledge base is the onboarding document that agents can also read — shared context accelerates both
A well-maintained CLAUDE.md means an agent can hit the ground running without one-on-one briefing
Shared context = faster ramp for humans and agents alike — you only write the answer once

slides.title: Agent Governance
slides.subtitle: Access, logging, review, and deprecation — the same rigour as code
points: Permission scoping: least-privilege access per role and context — an agent should only reach what it needs for that task
MCP access logging: what agents retrieve and when — visibility into what your agents are actually doing
Change review: prompt and config changes go through the same process as code — treat a bad prompt like a bad deploy
Deprecation: stale context is worse than no context — remove what no longer reflects reality
slides.tip: An ungoverned agent with broad access is a liability. The same instincts that drive good security engineering apply here.

slides.title: Context Quality Over Quantity
slides.subtitle: Every word in CLAUDE.md costs something — curate for signal, not coverage
points: Agents read CLAUDE.md at session start — bloated context degrades performance before the first prompt
Context rot at the org level: too much noise in shared config degrades every agent session, not just yours
Curate for signal: include what agents consistently get wrong without it; remove what they consistently get right regardless
The best shared context is short, precise, and never needs to be repeated in a prompt
slides.tip: If you haven't had to look at a CLAUDE.md line in a month, consider whether it's still earning its place.

slides.title: Compounding the Flywheel
slides.subtitle: One improvement to shared context benefits every agent session across the org
points: In individual AI use, the best workflows stay with the person who discovered them
In shared alignment, the best insight from your best prompt engineer reaches the whole team — without a meeting
Agent quality compounds with knowledge base quality — the investment pays dividends on every future session
This is the compounding advantage that organisations can have that individuals cannot

slides.title: Anti-Patterns to Avoid
slides.subtitle: The most common ways organisational AI alignment fails
points: Prompt silos: each team hoards their best workflows — productivity gains don't spread, the org can't learn
Undifferentiated context dumps into CLAUDE.md: everything added, nothing curated — context rot at scale
Ungoverned MCP access without logging or scoping: agents reach everything, visibility is zero
Knowledge base as archive, not living system: stale context actively misleads agents and confuses new hires
Treating agent alignment as an IT project: it's 80% people and knowledge, 20% infrastructure

slides.title: The Hard Questions
slides.subtitle: What most organisations haven't figured out yet
points: Who owns the knowledge base? If nobody owns it, it will rot. If one team owns it, it won't reflect the whole org.
How do teams contribute domain-specific context without fragmenting the shared standard?
Where does agent output go back into the knowledge base? Agents surface knowledge — if it disappears after the session, nothing compounds.
These aren't technical questions. They're organisational design questions.
slides.tip: Start with ownership. Everything else is easier once someone is accountable for keeping the knowledge base alive.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
slides.subtitle: What to carry out of this session
points: Individual AI adoption doesn't compound — distribute alignment through plugin, repo, and MCP layers
The knowledge base is the onboarding document both new hires and agents can read — invest in it once, benefit on every session
Context quality beats context quantity — curate aggressively, deprecate stale entries, treat the knowledge base as a living system
Agent governance is the same discipline as code governance: least privilege, logging, review, deprecation
The compounding flywheel: one improvement to shared context benefits every agent session across the org
The hardest question is organisational, not technical: who owns the knowledge base?
